title: Workbook — Respect for Human Life
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in groups. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What is the fifth commandment and how does Jesus deepen it in the Sermon on the Mount?
  2. Why does the Church teach that only God is the Lord of life? Explain in your own words.
  3. What is the difference between legitimate self-defense and revenge, according to the Catechism?
  4. Why does the Church oppose abortion from the moment of conception? What does science say about this?
  5. What distinguishes ordinary from extraordinary means when caring for a terminally ill patient?
  6. What does the Catechism say about the salvation of persons who have taken their own lives (CCC 2283)?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "right" answers to these personal questions.

1. Read Luke 10:25-37 (the Good Samaritan parable). Which character do you identify with most: the Samaritan, the priest/Levite, or the wounded man? Why?

Think of a real situation where you passed by. Or where someone was a Samaritan to you.

 

 

 

2. Jesus says that insulting someone is already a form of killing in the heart (Mt 5:22). Who have you "killed" with your words or contempt? What would it cost you to apologize?

 

 

 

3. Saint Gianna Beretta Molla chose her daughter's life at the cost of her own. Do you know someone who made a major sacrifice to defend life? Would you be capable of the same?

 

 

 

4. Of the threats to life mentioned in the lesson (abortion, euthanasia, suicide, scandal, death penalty), which hits closest to home? Have you witnessed any of these situations in your family or among friends?

 

 

 

5. Jesus commands love of enemies (Mt 5:44). Is there someone you consider an "enemy" or a "difficult person" in your life? What concrete action could you take this week to show them active love, even if you do not feel it?

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Write Your Own Good Samaritan Parable

Imagine Jesus tells the parable today. Who would be the wounded person: a migrant, a pregnant woman in crisis, an abandoned elderly person, a bullied classmate, an addict on the street? Who would be the priest and Levite of today? Who would be the unexpected Samaritan —someone no one would expect to help? Write your version (about 15-20 lines) and then share it with someone.

 

 

 

 

 

 

B. Letter to Saint Gianna

Imagine you can write a letter to Saint Gianna Beretta Molla. What would you ask her? What would you thank her for? What would you ask her to intercede for —for you or for someone you know? Write the letter with sincerity.

 

 

 

 

 

C. Map of Life

Draw a timeline of a human life, from conception to natural death. At each stage (conception, gestation, birth, childhood, adolescence, youth, adulthood, old age, illness, death), write how this life is threatened today and how it can be protected. Example: Gestation → Threat: abortion → Protection: pregnancy help centers, accompaniment, adoption.

 

 

 

D. Three Actions for Life This Week

Commit to three concrete actions for life this week. Ideas: visit an elderly family member, pray for pregnant women in crisis, donate to a pro-life pregnancy center, write a letter to a political representative about life issues, smile and talk to the person who cleans your street or collects your trash (remembering that every life is the image of God), reconcile with someone. Write your three commitments:

1. _______________________________________________

2. _______________________________________________

3. _______________________________________________

Scripture to Memorize

"Go and do likewise."

— Luke 10:37

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, Good Samaritan of my soul,
teach me to see every person as you see them:
with infinite dignity, loved by the Father from all eternity.
Heal my heart of contempt, of resentment, of indifference.
Give me a merciful heart that stops before the one who suffers,
even if they are not one of my own, even if it costs me.
Saint Gianna Beretta Molla, intercede for me
that I may never fear to defend life.
Amen.

Print Workbook
